questions.question: Briefly explain the two main steps involved in running a new program in a UNIX environment, naming the relevant system calls.
keyPoints: Use the fork() system call to create a new child process which is a copy of the parent.
The child process then uses a member of the exec() family of system calls to replace its memory space with the new program.

questions.question: Explain the following terms in the context of process management: Zombie Process, Orphan Process, and Cascading Termination. How does an operating system typically handle orphaned processes?
keyPoints: Zombie Process: A terminated process whose PCB still exists because the parent has not yet read its exit status via wait().
Orphan Process: A child process whose parent has terminated before it did.
Cascading Termination: A policy where the termination of a parent process forces the termination of all its child processes.

Handling Orphans: Modern UNIX-like systems reassign orphaned processes to be children of the init process (PID 1), which periodically calls wait() to reap them and prevent them from becoming permanent zombies.
